title: The Cartan Geometry of the Rotating Kepler Problem
speaker: Otto van Koert
abstract: GEOMETRY/DYNAMICAL SYSTEMSIn this talk we shall discuss the Cartan geometry of the rotating Kepler problem. The rotating Kepler problem appears as the limit of the restricted planar three-body body when one of the masses goes to zero. As such, this problem plays the role of a simple approximation. We shall discuss the Cartan curvature and some of its relations with indices in the three-body problem. This is joint work with Kai Cieliebak and Urs Frauenfelder.

proof_stages.label: Conley-Zehnder indices as foliation dimension data
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by explaining that Conley-Zehnder indices enter the Fredholm dimension formula for moduli spaces of punctured holomorphic curves, so index 3 orbits can supply the two-dimensional families needed for finite-energy foliations and global surfaces of section.

proof_stages.label: Index-two obstruction in the three-body neck
proof_stages.summary: The speaker motivates dynamic convexity by describing how, above the Lagrange points in the restricted three-body problem, index-two halo orbits in the connected-sum neck can cause holomorphic-plane families to break, so one wants to exclude further index-two orbits near the heavy body.

proof_stages.label: Finsler and Cartan reformulation of index computation
proof_stages.summary: Instead of finding every periodic orbit and linearizing the flow, the speaker proposes estimating indices through geodesic-flow geometry, introducing Finsler metrics on tangent bundles and their dual Cartan metrics on cotangent bundles.

proof_stages.label: Moser-regularized rotating Kepler Cartan metric
proof_stages.summary: For the rotating Kepler problem at mass parameter zero, the Hamiltonian is regularized on a fixed energy level, a fiber-scaling equation is solved to obtain an explicit nonreversible Cartan metric, and the joint result is stated that this metric is Cartan and yields nonnegative geodesic indices.

proof_stages.label: Curvature criterion for dynamic convexity
proof_stages.summary: To upgrade nonnegative indices to Conley-Zehnder index at least 3, the speaker invokes a Harris-Paternain type theorem using positive Gauss curvature and a lower bound on shortest geodesic length, explained through the canonical coframing and the resulting linearized-flow matrix.

proof_stages.label: Negative curvature and failure of this method
proof_stages.summary: The computed Gauss curvature of the rotating Kepler Cartan metric becomes negative below the Lagrange level, so the curvature criterion cannot prove dynamic convexity, although direct index computations still show dynamic convexity below the Lagrange point and may persist for small mass.

examples.name: Standard S3 finite-energy foliation toy model
examples.note: The Hopf/Reeb flow on standard S3, viewed as a double cover of geodesic flow on S2, is used to show why a Conley-Zehnder index of 3 gives a two-dimensional family of holomorphic planes suitable for a foliation.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the S3 holomorphic-plane foliation, projects it, checks the moduli dimension/index relation, and identifies CZ index 3 for the toy orbit

examples.name: Connected-sum three-body neck with halo orbit
examples.note: The above-Lagrange connected-sum picture with a halo/Lyapunov orbit of index 2 motivates the need to rule out additional index-two orbits near the heavy body because such orbits could break the foliation.

examples.name: Slow twisting trajectories near the Lagrange point
